article_title: "I started to lose my ability to code": Developers grapple with the real cost of AI programming tools
core_thesis: AI coding tools like Claude Code and LLMs are transforming software development, reigniting passion and productivity for senior programmers while raising serious concerns about skill atrophy, code quality, and the erosion of the junior developer pipeline. The article presents a balanced view of the trade-offs, highlighting both the euphoria of accelerated development and the risks of over-reliance on AI.
detailed_summary: The article, published by The New Stack, explores the mixed reactions of developers to increasingly powerful AI coding tools. It draws on a New York Times essay by Paul Ford, who celebrates the ability to quickly revive old projects, and a Times magazine piece by Clive Thompson, who interviewed over 70 developers at major tech companies. Many reported being 'weirdly jazzed' about their new powers, but concerns emerged. Pia Torain, a software engineer, admitted she 'started to lose my ability to code' after months of heavy AI use, warning that skills atrophy if not practiced. This echoes a broader anxiety: if junior developers aren't hired because LLMs can do their work, how will they gain expertise? The article also covers a Hacker News thread where a 60-year-old programmer said Claude Code 're-ignited a passion,' drawing 989 comments. Senior developers like Steve Yegge report 10x-100x productivity gains but also note exhaustion and disrupted sleep. Others, like Joel Dare, complain about AI-generated code quality—e.g., 89 dependencies in a Node project. The piece concludes that AI tools amplify existing expertise but may undermine long-term skill development, especially for juniors.

key_data_points: Pia Torain, a software engineer, reported that after four months of issuing hundreds of prompts a day, she 'started to lose my ability to code' and now makes a conscious effort to absorb the program's architecture. (🟢 Relevant to your programming: highlights the risk of skill atrophy if you rely too heavily on AI without practicing fundamentals.)

A Hacker News commenter noted: 'If nobody’s hiring junior devs because LLMs can do junior work faster and cheaper, how is anyone going to become an expert?' (🟢 Relevant to your student status: underscores the importance of deliberate practice and building deep expertise early in your career.)

Steve Yegge reported being '10x, 20x, even 100 times as productive' but also experienced disrupted sleep cycles due to high-level decision-making exhaustion. (🟢 Relevant to your ambition: shows extreme productivity gains are possible but come with cognitive costs.)

Joel Dare complained that after forgetting to tell Claude not to use frameworks, he ended up with 89 dependencies in a Node project, calling it 'architectural decay.' (🟢 Relevant to your programming: illustrates the need to guide AI tools carefully to avoid technical debt.)

Kent Beck, a 64-year-old programming guru, said LLMs got him programming again after a decade, describing AI's unpredictability as 'addictive, in a slot-machine way.' (🟢 Relevant to your interests: shows AI can re-engage experienced developers but may foster dependency.)

A Hacker News commenter stated: 'LLMs are quite good at coding, but terrible at software engineering… the overall application is a dog’s breakfast of spaghetti.' (🟢 Relevant to your Red Teaming: poor code quality from AI can introduce vulnerabilities and maintenance nightmares.)

Chris Marshall, 63, said LLMs allowed him to 'dream big, again' and addressed the isolation of retirement. (🟢 Relevant to your homelab/devops: AI can serve as a collaborative partner for solo projects.)
conclusions: AI coding tools provide significant productivity boosts, especially for senior developers, but risk skill atrophy for those who over-rely on them. Developers should balance AI assistance with deliberate practice to maintain core coding abilities.

Code quality concerns are real: AI-generated code often works at the function level but lacks architectural coherence, leading to technical debt and potential security issues. Developers must actively guide and review AI output.

The junior developer pipeline is threatened: if LLMs replace entry-level tasks, new programmers may struggle to gain the experience needed to become experts. This has implications for the entire software engineering profession.

The emotional response to AI tools is polarized: some find renewed passion and reduced isolation, while others experience burnout and loss of craft satisfaction. The long-term impact on developer well-being and career trajectories remains uncertain.
personal_relevance_summary: This article is highly relevant to you as a programmer and student because it directly addresses the trade-offs of using AI coding tools—a topic you'll encounter in your studies and career. For your Red Teaming interests, the article highlights that AI-generated code can introduce architectural decay and excessive dependencies, which are potential attack surfaces. As a student, the warning about skill atrophy is crucial: you should continue to practice coding fundamentals manually to build deep expertise. The article also offers insights for your homelab/devops projects: AI can help you revive abandoned projects or act as a pair programmer, but you must maintain oversight to avoid technical debt. Overall, it provides a balanced perspective that can inform how you integrate AI into your workflow without losing your edge.
extra_advice: Consider setting aside dedicated 'no-AI' coding sessions each week to deliberately practice problem-solving and architecture design. For your Red Teaming practice, use AI to generate code snippets and then manually audit them for vulnerabilities—this will sharpen both your offensive security skills and your ability to critique AI output. Additionally, experiment with AI tools on side projects to understand their strengths and weaknesses firsthand, but always review and refactor the generated code to maintain quality.
